title: City of Chaos: Introduction
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Om4MsEtTrnw

Daniel (@dungeondive) kicks off his very first Let's Play series with City of Chaos — a long-sought "holy grail" game, made in the late 1990s, finally snagged at a decent price on eBay. He frames it less as a board game than a work of interactive fiction with components: a few pages of deliberately ambiguous rules (the designers themselves left much to player interpretation on BoardGameGeek), a vast Tome of Chaos paragraph book, location and action decks, and the strikingly bizarre, otherworldly miniatures he's painted (christened "Bing" and "Dizzy").

You explore the cursed city of Burynathar, drawing location tiles and cards as the city sprawls across the table, interacting with NPCs by greeting, helping or threatening them, and chasing clue cards toward one of several plot resolutions. Combat is a simple attack/defend card pick. He'll play solo with two characters under house rules to keep a five-hour game nearer three.

The heart of his enthusiasm is literary: the game's weird, mysterious vibe reminds him powerfully of author Michael Cisko (The Divinity Student), whose puzzle-like weird fiction he adores. He promises a spoiler-heavy, hopefully definitive playthrough, since almost nothing exists online.

----

Have you ever hunted down a "holy grail" game for years — and did the reality live up to the long chase once it finally hit your table?
